assignment_description:
Sir Roger de Coverley

PREP: Watch instructional video to familiarize yourself with dance. Print Sir Roger de Coverley Instructions PDF.

➜ INTRO
Sir Roger de Coverley is an English Country Dance originally published in _The Dancing Master_ in 1695. It was widely danced in the 1700s and remained popular through the 1800s. It is most famously found in Charles Dickens', _A Christmas Carol,_ at Fezziwig's ball. It is a finishing dance that was commonly danced at the end of a ball where all attendees partnered up to close out the evening. It promoted good humor among the company and caused them all to leave having great satisfaction with one another. After the dance crossed the ocean and came over to America, it morphed into different variations and also became what we now know at the Virginia Reel. You will recognize the structure and formations as they have similarities.

➜ VIEW
Watch the example of Sir Roger de Coverley. \*Please note that there are many versions of Sir Roger de Coverley. Our example video differs from our instructional video.\*
∞ Video Link: Sir Roger de Coverley

➜ VIEW & PRACTICE
Using Instructional Video, form pairs, designate diagonals, and learn Part 1 (meet in middle, right hand turn, left hand turn) without music. Instructor will need to cue dancers and count in 6's.
\*It is often helpful for the students to remain with the same partner week to week while learning the piece. Once mastered, they can change partners.\*
∞ Video Link: Instructional Sir Roger de Coverley
∞ Sir Roger de Coverley Instructions PDF

• EXTRA HELPING

The entire Part 1 together without music.

Sir Roger de Coverley

➜ REVIEW

➜ LISTEN
To Sir Roger de Coverley music. While listening, practice clapping together to a portion of the music and then counting together by 6's. Go back to the beginning and practice counting the 6 count introduction so students learn the intro begins right away.

➜ PRACTICE
Parts 1 and 2 together with music. Instructor must call dancers in and say, "Remember to honor your partner (press play) 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, Go!" \*It is helpful if instructor counts by 6's during dance ensuring students go in for 6 and out for 6.\*

assignment_description:
Capoeira

➜ PREP
Watch instructional video to familiarize yourself with warm up and dance. Print or have a digital copy of Capoeira PDF while teaching Capoeira.

➜ INTRO
Capoeira History: “Capoeira is a Brazilian art form that developed from combat games enslaved Africans brought to Brazil during the colonial period. It features dance, martial arts, music, songs, acrobatics, and, many would say, a particular philosophy. In performance practitioners form a circle ("roda") where eight of them play instruments creating a groove that supports songs and the physical action. Songs are in Portuguese and the instruments are all percussion, with the exception of the berimbaus, three musical bows that lead the musical ensemble. Players take turns to come to the center of the circle in couples for the physical action. These physical encounters are known as jogos (games), neither a dance, nor a combat. During the performance, sometimes players seem to be dancing and other times to be fighting. This ambiguity is integral to capoeira and practitioners strive to balance the two. With a legacy linked to resistance and liberation from Portuguese colonizers and enslavers, many practitioners nowadays see capoeira as a symbol of cultural resistance and an instrument to educate and raise awareness about racial inequality. These views have been multiplied since the 1980s when capoeira became globalized.”  - Mestre Cobra Mansa (recognized Capoeira Angola master based in Brazil)

➜ VIEW
Example of Capoeira.
∞ Video Link: Capoeira 1
∞ Video Link: Capoeira 2

Capoeira

➜ WARM UP & REVIEW
Complete Warm Up and review Ginga, Role, Au, Esquiva Lateral, Esquiva Baixa, Meia Lua de Compassa, Armada, Double Kick, and Queixada.
∞ Warm Up Music (begin at 08:34 - 17:03)

Capoeira is performed as an improvisational, non-choreographed duel between two people in a “roda” (circle) where they escape movement without touch or resistance.  At the cue of the lead instrument (the berimbau), the two players begin by shaking hands or sharing a hug to show respect and affirm the “fight” is a friendly game.  Movements are spontaneous without a fixed pattern and a movement dialogue is created.  Though Capoeira is not performed like a set memorized dance, for educational purposes we have put together the steps we have learned into a dance.  As you become familiar with some of the steps, you may pair up with classmates and slowly and carefully practice these moves spontaneously in pairs at your own timing.  Remember, you are not trying to hurt your partner but create a dialogue of movement where one seeks to outmaneuver the other.  Movements should be executed with care and control.  If you want to explore Capoeira further, seek out in-person lessons in your area.
